Wellness Policy Assessment Tool and Report Template


Included in the
written policy?

Yes    No


Implemented in the
school building(s)?

Fully in
Place     Partially
in Place     Not in
Place


We limit the number of food fundraisers at school and have procedures in place
for requesting a fundraiser exemption (i.e., selling foods that do not meet Smart
Snacks in accordance with limits set by PDE.

We have local standards in our written policy for foods and beverages
offered for free to students at school, including food rewards, items offered
at classroom parties and celebrations, and foods/beverages provided to the
class as shared classroom snacks.

We provide a list of nonfood ideas and healthy food/beverage alternatives to staff
and parents/guardians.

Only foods and beverages that meet or exceed federal nutrition standards
(USDA Smart Snacks in School) are permitted to be marketed or promoted
to students during the school day. Examples: posters, vending machines,
menu boards, cups for beverage dispensing, coolers, trash cans.

Notes on nutrition guidelines for foods and beverages at school:


* At least one goal for these categories must be included in the written policy per federal regulations.


Report on the progress made in attaining the goals of the wellness policy (REQUIRED):


July 2018                          This institution is an equal opportunity provider                          5

answers.field_label: Notes On Nutrition Guidelines For Foods And Beverages At School
answers.value:
Persona: 
I am filling this out as Dr. Melissa A. Carter, Wellness Coordinator for Brookside Unified School District in Columbus, Ohio. I have a background in public health nutrition (MPH, RD) and have been in this district role since 2019 after previously working as a middle school health teacher and a clinical dietitian. Our district serves about 4,800 students across six schools (four elementary, one middle, one high school), with approximately 58% of students eligible for free or reduced-price meals. Many of our families work multiple jobs, and access to affordable, healthy food is a recurring concern raised at PTA meetings and in our school climate surveys.

Context for our nutrition guidelines and how they operate in real life:
1. Limiting food fundraisers and fundraiser exemptions
- We do limit the number of food-based fundraisers during the school day, and this is written into Board Policy 8505 and our Local School Wellness Policy Administrative Guidelines.
- Each school is allowed a maximum of three food-based fundraiser exemption days per school year where items that do not meet Smart Snacks standards may be sold (for example, the high school band’s annual donut sale or the senior class bake sale). These days must be approved in advance.
- We have a formal procedure: clubs or parent groups submit a "Fundraiser Request Form" to the building principal at least four weeks in advance. The form includes a section where they indicate whether the fundraiser is a food fundraiser and whether the foods meet Smart Snacks standards. If they are requesting an exemption, the principal forwards the request to me (Wellness Coordinator) and the Food Service Director for review.
- In practice, principals are careful about not clustering exemptions too close together. For instance, at Brookside High School last year, we approved one exemption in October (homecoming week), one in February (winter formal week), and one in May (senior week). We denied a fourth request from the robotics club and helped them convert the fundraiser to a nonfood option (selling school-branded phone wallets and water bottles instead).
- We also encourage groups to schedule food-based fundraisers after the end of the school day whenever possible. For example, the middle school PTO moved its ice cream social fundraiser to 5:30–7:00 p.m. so that it would not conflict with the instructional day or be considered a Smart Snacks issue.

2. Local standards for foods and beverages offered for free
- Our written policy includes clear local standards for any food or beverages offered for free to students, such as classroom rewards, birthday celebrations, holiday parties, and shared snacks.
- Key elements of our local standards:
  • Foods must either meet Smart Snacks guidelines or be on our district-approved “Healthy Celebrations and Rewards” list.
  • Sugary beverages (including soda and energy drinks) are not allowed to be provided to students at any time during the school day, even if they are free.
  • We strongly discourage candy-only rewards. Teachers are encouraged to use nonfood rewards first (extra recess, homework passes, classroom privileges) and to limit food rewards to no more than two times per month.
  • For elementary birthday celebrations, we promote nonfood celebrations such as bringing in a favorite book to be read aloud or donating a game to the classroom. If families still choose to send food, it must be store-bought, individually wrapped, and nut-free, and we encourage items like fruit cups, mini muffins, or yogurt tubes.
- These standards are communicated in multiple ways:
  • Included in the student/parent handbook (Section: Health & Wellness)
  • Sent home every August in a one-page flyer titled "Healthy Celebrations at Brookside"
  • Reviewed with staff at the August professional development days and again in January refresher meetings
- A concrete example: At Brookside Elementary, teachers used to have cupcakes for almost every birthday, sometimes multiple times a week. Since implementing the local standards, our school counselor worked with teachers to develop a "Birthday Book Club" where students choose a book to donate to the classroom library with a nameplate. Teachers report that this has reduced disruptions and sugar highs, and parents appreciate not feeling pressured to send in elaborate treats.

3. Providing lists of nonfood ideas and healthy alternatives
- We do provide staff and parents/guardians with detailed lists of nonfood reward ideas and healthier food and beverage alternatives. I personally update these lists every summer.
- The nonfood reward list includes over 50 ideas grouped by grade level, such as:
  • Extra recess or "walk and talk" laps
  • Sit with a friend at lunch
  • Line leader or classroom helper for the day
  • Positive phone call or email home
  • Choose the class brain break activity or song
  • "Teacher’s chair" privilege for one lesson
  • Free homework pass or project extension coupon (within reason)
- The healthy foods and beverages list includes examples that meet Smart Snacks standards and are realistic for families on a budget, such as:
  • Fresh fruit (apples, bananas, clementines, grapes)
  • Vegetable sticks with hummus or low-fat dip
  • Whole-grain crackers, pretzels, or popcorn (lightly salted, no added butter)
  • Low-fat string cheese or yogurt cups
  • Water, unflavored low-fat milk, or 100% fruit juice in small portions
- Distribution and communication:
  • These lists are posted on the district website under “Wellness & Nutrition.”
  • We print copies for back-to-school nights and PTO meetings.
  • They are emailed to all staff at the beginning of each semester.
  • Our school nurses keep extra copies in their offices and often hand them out when meeting with parents of students with nutrition-related health concerns.
- A practical example: The 6th grade team at Brookside Middle School used to host a "Pizza Party" for students who met reading goals. After reviewing our guidelines and the nonfood rewards list, they shifted to a "Game & Extra Recess Celebration" with optional fruit and popcorn. Students actually reported in our wellness survey that they preferred the games and extra recess over the pizza.

4. Marketing of foods and beverages during the school day
- Our policy states that only foods and beverages that meet or exceed federal nutrition standards (USDA Smart Snacks) may be marketed or promoted to students during the school day.
- This includes:
  • Vending machine fronts and labels
  • Posters, digital signage, and bulletin boards in cafeterias and hallways
  • Scoreboard displays and signage in gyms and athletic fields used during the school day
  • School store offerings and promotions
- We conducted an initial "marketing audit" in fall 2022. We found:
  • Several old soda-branded scoreboards in the high school gym and on the football field. While we could not immediately replace the scoreboards due to cost, we placed district-branded magnetic covers over the soda logos during the school day and for all school-sponsored events. We are budgeting to replace the boards over the next two years with non-branded equipment.
  • A few posters from a previous contract with a sports drink company in the high school weight room and near the concession stand. These were removed and replaced with posters promoting water and physical activity.
  • Vending machines were re-wrapped with generic "Fuel for Learning" graphics instead of brand-specific advertisements.
- Our food service management company is contractually required to ensure that any promotional materials they bring on campus (e.g., posters, table tents) feature only items that meet Smart Snacks standards. We review these materials quarterly.

5. Implementation challenges and ongoing work
- While our written policies are strong and broadly implemented, there are still some areas of partial compliance:
  • Classroom practices can vary: Some teachers still occasionally use candy as a quick reward, especially around holidays. We are addressing this through additional training and by sharing teacher-to-teacher success stories about nonfood rewards that work.
  • Community events: Even though our policy technically applies only during the school day, families sometimes assume that after-school events sponsored by the school (like the fall festival) follow the same guidelines. We are working to clarify communications so that parents understand when Smart Snacks rules apply and when they do not, while still encouraging healthier options at all times.
  • Cultural celebrations: We want to be respectful of cultural food traditions while also maintaining our nutrition standards. For example, during Hispanic Heritage Month, families wanted to bring in homemade tamales and pan dulce. We worked with them to schedule these tastings after the official school day and to pair them with educational components about culture and history.

6. Monitoring and feedback
- We monitor implementation through:
  • Annual wellness policy assessments at each school
  • Cafeteria observations conducted twice per year
  • A short parent survey included in our spring climate survey
  • Student focus groups at the middle and high school levels
- Feedback so far has been largely positive. In parent comments, some families initially felt the birthday celebration changes were "too strict," but many have since noted that it reduces pressure on them to provide sweets and large quantities of food. Teachers have reported fewer behavior issues tied to high-sugar classroom parties.

7. Summary
Overall, our district has moved from a more informal, inconsistent approach to classroom food and fundraising toward a clearly articulated, health-promoting system grounded in federal nutrition standards and local guidelines. While there is still work to do in achieving 100% consistency, especially around classroom practices and special events, we have made significant progress in aligning our day-to-day practices with the written wellness policy and in communicating expectations to staff, students, and families.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Report On The Progress Made In Attaining The Goals Of The Wellness Policy
answers.value:
This narrative is submitted by Dr. Melissa A. Carter, District Wellness Coordinator for Brookside Unified School District in Columbus, Ohio, for the 2025–2026 school year wellness policy progress report.

1. Overview of wellness policy goals
Our district wellness policy focuses on four main goal areas:
1) Improving the nutritional quality of foods and beverages available to students during the school day.
2) Increasing opportunities for physical activity and reducing sedentary time.
3) Enhancing nutrition education and health literacy across grade levels.
4) Strengthening family and community engagement around wellness.

The sections below summarize progress made in each area during the past two school years (2023–2024 and 2024–2025), with particular emphasis on the most recent year.

2. Progress on nutrition environment and food quality
2.1 School meals
- Participation in school meals:
  • Average daily participation in the National School Lunch Program increased from 64% in 2022–2023 to 69% in 2024–2025.
  • Breakfast participation increased from 31% to 38%, largely due to expanding "grab-and-go" breakfast kiosks at the middle and high school.
- Menu improvements:
  • We added at least one fresh fruit option daily at all schools (previously, some days relied solely on canned fruit). For example, Brookside Middle School now offers fresh apples, oranges, or seasonal fruit (such as clementines or melon) every day.
  • We increased the use of whole grains, replacing white pasta with whole-grain pasta and switching to brown rice in most recipes.
  • Sodium has been reduced in several popular items by switching to lower-sodium deli meats and using more herbs and spices instead of salt.
- Student feedback:
  • In a spring 2025 survey of 1,127 students in grades 4–12, 72% reported that they are "satisfied" or "very satisfied" with school lunch options, up from 59% in the 2023 survey.
  • Students specifically noted they appreciate the build-your-own salad and taco bars that were piloted at the high school and middle school.

2.2 Competitive foods and Smart Snacks compliance
- Vending and à la carte:
  • All vending machines accessible to students during the school day now carry only Smart Snacks-compliant items. This was verified in our March 2025 internal audit.
  • High-sugar beverages (regular soda, energy drinks) have been removed from student-accessible vending machines. Machines in staff-only areas are clearly labeled and not accessible to students.
- Classroom snacks and celebrations:
  • At the elementary level, 3 of 4 schools report that at least 80% of classroom celebrations now follow the district’s "Healthy Celebrations" guidelines (based on principal reports and teacher surveys). The fourth school is at approximately 65% compliance and is receiving additional support.
  • Teachers report a noticeable decrease in candy-based rewards. In our 2025 staff survey, 68% of teachers said they "rarely" or "never" use candy as a reward, compared to 44% in 2022.

2.3 Fundraisers and food marketing
- Fundraisers:
  • Each school stayed within the district limit of three exempt food fundraisers per year during the 2024–2025 school year.
  • The number of nonfood fundraisers increased (e.g., walk-a-thons, read-a-thons, spirit wear sales). PTO leaders report that nonfood fundraisers are often easier to manage and can be just as profitable.
- Marketing:
  • All visible soda and energy drink marketing has been removed or covered in student areas during the school day. This includes gym scoreboards (temporarily covered with school-branded magnets) and posters.
  • Cafeteria marketing now focuses on healthy choices, such as promoting water, fruits, vegetables, and whole grains.

3. Progress on physical activity
3.1 Physical education (PE)
- Elementary schools:
  • All four elementary schools meet the district guideline of at least 90 minutes of PE per week for students in grades 1–5.
  • Kindergarten students receive at least 60 minutes of structured PE plus daily recess.
- Middle and high school:
  • The middle school requires one semester of PE for each of grades 6–8, plus opportunities for intramural sports and activity clubs.
  • The high school continues to require one full PE credit for graduation. We have added more diverse course offerings, including yoga, fitness walking, and strength training, to appeal to students who are not interested in traditional team sports.

3.2 Recess and classroom activity breaks
- Recess:
  • All elementary schools provide at least 20 minutes of daily recess, with three schools consistently providing 25–30 minutes.
  • We have emphasized that recess should not be withheld as a punishment, and this expectation is included in the staff handbook. While occasional exceptions still occur, principals are monitoring this and addressing patterns with individual teachers.
- Classroom activity breaks:
  • At the elementary level, approximately 75% of teachers report that they incorporate short movement or brain breaks at least once per day.
  • At the middle school, several teachers are piloting "walk and talk" discussions and standing workstations.

3.3 After-school and extracurricular physical activity
- We added two new after-school clubs in 2024–2025: a walking club at the middle school and a "Beginner Fitness" club at the high school that focuses on basic strength and cardio workouts.
- Participation in intramural sports at the middle school increased by 18% compared to the previous year, partly due to offering non-competitive options like "just for fun" basketball and open gym.

4. Progress on nutrition education and health literacy
4.1 Curriculum integration
- Elementary:
  • Nutrition education lessons are incorporated into science and health units at all grade levels. For example, 3rd graders complete a "MyPlate" unit where they plan a balanced meal using cut-and-paste food images.
- Middle school:
  • 7th grade health classes include a three-week unit on reading food labels, understanding added sugar, and recognizing marketing tactics aimed at youth.
  • Students complete a project where they compare the nutrition facts of two similar foods (e.g., flavored yogurt vs. plain yogurt with fruit) and present their findings.
- High school:
  • The required health course includes a section on nutrition, food access, and chronic disease prevention.
  • An elective "Food & Fitness" course was introduced in 2024–2025 and has been fully enrolled both semesters. Students learn basic cooking skills, meal planning on a budget, and how nutrition relates to athletic performance.

4.2 Staff training
- We provided two district-wide professional development sessions focused on:
  • Implementing nonfood rewards and healthy celebrations.
  • Recognizing and responding to disordered eating patterns in students, with guidance on when to refer to the school counselor or nurse.
- Feedback surveys indicate that 82% of staff found the training "helpful" or "very helpful." Several teachers specifically mentioned the practical classroom strategies and scripts for talking about food in a non-judgmental way.

5. Family and community engagement
5.1 Communication with families
- We have improved communication about wellness through:
  • A quarterly "Healthy Brookside" newsletter emailed to families and posted on the district website.
  • Regular social media posts highlighting school garden activities, healthy cafeteria options, and student wellness projects.
  • Inclusion of wellness information (such as healthy breakfast ideas and hydration tips) in back-to-school packets.

5.2 Family events and partnerships
- We hosted two "Family Wellness Nights" (one at the middle school in fall and one at the high school in spring) with cooking demonstrations, fitness mini-classes, and information tables from local health organizations.
- We partnered with a local hospital system and a community health center to provide:
  • Free blood pressure screenings at parent-teacher conference nights.
  • Nutrition counseling referrals for families requesting additional support.

6. Equity and access considerations
- Recognizing that many of our families face economic challenges, we have focused on ensuring that wellness initiatives do not place additional financial or time burdens on parents.
- Examples:
  • Our "Healthy Celebrations" guidelines emphasize simple, low-cost options (e.g., bananas, popcorn, water) and nonfood celebrations, so families are not expected to bring elaborate treats.
  • We expanded the school breakfast program and promoted it through flyers that explicitly mention that breakfast is available regardless of income status.
  • For after-school physical activity clubs, we work to keep participation fees either very low or fully waived, using grant funds where possible.

7. Data and evaluation
- We use multiple data sources to monitor progress:
  • Annual wellness policy self-assessment tools completed by each school.
  • Student, parent, and staff surveys conducted in spring 2025.
  • Participation data from school meals and after-school programs.
  • Feedback from principals, school nurses, and counselors.
- Key findings:
  • Students report feeling more aware of healthy food choices, although taste and cost still heavily influence their decisions.
  • Parents appreciate the clearer guidelines around classroom food but would like more recipes and practical tips for healthy, budget-friendly snacks.
  • Teachers generally support the policy but have requested more ready-to-use lesson materials that tie directly into state standards.

8. Challenges and areas for improvement
- Consistency in classroom implementation: While our policies are strong on paper, actual practices still vary by classroom and building. We need to provide ongoing training, simple checklists, and more principal-level monitoring.
- Time constraints: Teachers and food service staff are stretched thin. We need to continue to streamline wellness-related tasks (e.g., simplifying forms, providing ready-made resources) so that implementation feels manageable.
- Student preferences: Some students still prefer highly processed, salty, or sugary foods, and it takes time for taste preferences to shift. We are exploring more taste tests, student-led menu planning, and culturally diverse healthy options.
- Facilities: Some older buildings have limited kitchen and storage space, which can make fresh food preparation more challenging. We are working with the district facilities department to prioritize kitchen upgrades in our capital improvement plan.

9. Next steps and goals for the coming year
For the 2025–2026 school year, our specific wellness goals include:
1) Increase breakfast participation to at least 42% district-wide by expanding grab-and-go options and piloting "second chance" breakfast at the high school.
2) Achieve at least 85% compliance with healthy classroom celebrations at all four elementary schools.
3) Develop and distribute a teacher-friendly "Wellness in the Classroom" toolkit with short, standards-aligned nutrition and physical activity mini-lessons.
4) Expand student voice in wellness by establishing student wellness committees at the middle and high school levels.
5) Strengthen data collection by standardizing our annual wellness assessment process and providing clearer feedback reports to each school.

10. Personal reflection as Wellness Coordinator
As someone who has worked both in clinical nutrition and in classrooms, I have seen firsthand how school environments can either support or undermine healthy habits. Over the past several years, I have also experienced this as a parent—my own two children attend Brookside schools (one in 3rd grade and one in 7th). I have noticed fewer days where they come home having had cupcakes and candy before lunch, and more days where they talk about trying new fruits or participating in "Fun Friday Fitness" activities.

While we are not perfect, I am proud of the progress we have made as a district in aligning our daily practices with the intent of our wellness policy. The culture is slowly shifting toward one where healthy eating and active living are seen as normal parts of the school experience, rather than add-ons or special events. With continued collaboration among administrators, teachers, food service staff, families, students, and community partners, I am confident we can continue to move closer to fully realizing the goals of our wellness policy in the coming years.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

persona.education: BS in Nutrition and Dietetics (Ohio State University, 2005); Dietetic Internship (Ohio State Wexner Medical Center, 2006); Registered Dietitian (RD); Master of Public Health in Public Health Nutrition (Ohio State University, 2012); Ohio teaching license in Health Education.
family_members: David Carter (husband, 44, high school social studies teacher at Brookside High School)
Evan Carter (son, 13, 7th grader at Brookside Middle School)
Lily Carter (daughter, 9, 3rd grader at Brookside Elementary School)
Marjorie “Marge” Nelson (mother, 71, retired nurse, lives in Gahanna, OH)
Thomas Nelson (father, 73, retired electrician, lives in Gahanna, OH)
Rachel Nelson-Greer (younger sister, 39, pediatric nurse practitioner in Cincinnati, OH)
Mark Greer (brother-in-law, 40, IT consultant, lives in Cincinnati, OH)
persona.background_context:
This is Melissa, a working parent and public health professional juggling a district-level role with the realities of raising two kids in the same school system she helps shape. She and her husband David bought their modest three-bedroom split-level on Brookmeadow Drive in 2016, drawn by the tree-lined streets, neighborhood sidewalks, and the fact that the Brookside schools were close enough for the kids to bike when they were older. Most weekday mornings start around 5:45 a.m., when she sneaks in a 25-minute yoga or strength session in the basement before waking Evan and Lily at 6:30. Breakfast is usually simple—oatmeal, scrambled eggs, or whole-grain toast with peanut butter—and she quietly feels a sense of satisfaction when the kids opt to eat at school because she knows firsthand that the breakfast kiosks now offer decent, balanced options.

Professionally, Melissa has been the Wellness Coordinator for Brookside Unified School District since July 2019, after seven years as a middle school health teacher and four years as a clinical dietitian at a local hospital. Her office is in the district administration building just off West Broad Street in Columbus, but she spends a lot of time in the six school buildings—walking cafeterias with clipboards, chatting with principals about birthday celebrations, and checking in with PE teachers about how often they’re able to fit in movement breaks. In the last two years, she’s poured energy into expanding grab-and-go breakfast options and tightening up how classroom parties are handled. She’s proud that lunch participation is up and that vending machines no longer advertise soda, but she’s realistic about the gaps: she knows there are still teachers who keep a bag of candy in their desk drawer and that consistency across classrooms is a work in progress.

Life at home is busy but fairly stable. David teaches U.S. history and government at Brookside High School, and although they work in the same district, they rarely see each other during the school day; instead, their paths cross in the parking lot when she’s leaving a wellness committee meeting and he’s heading to coach mock trial or supervise a study hall. Evan, now in 7th grade, has recently joined the middle school walking club that Melissa helped launch, and he alternates between enthusiasm for trying new after-school activities and eye-rolling when she brings up nutrition. Lily, in 3rd grade, loves the “Birthday Book Club” at her school and proudly points out the nameplates on books donated in her classmates’ honor. Melissa quietly notes that Lily has had fewer sugar-fueled meltdowns after school since cupcakes stopped being a twice-weekly occurrence in the classroom.

Over the past year, Melissa’s days have been shaped by several ongoing projects and personal concerns. She’s working closely with the district’s food service director to plan for kitchen upgrades in the two oldest buildings, knowing that limited storage and equipment make it harder to offer fresh fruits and vegetables consistently. Once a month, she meets with a small group of student leaders at the high school to talk about menu ideas and wellness initiatives; she’s excited about their proposal for a student-led taste test featuring culturally diverse dishes that still meet Smart Snacks standards. At the same time, she’s aware of staff fatigue and tries to keep her asks practical—offering ready-made lesson snippets teachers can plug into existing units and simplifying the paperwork for clubs that want to run nonfood fundraisers.

At home, Melissa is navigating the normal worries of a parent with school-age kids. Evan is starting to care more about his body image and athletic performance, and she’s careful about how she talks about food—focusing on energy and strength rather than weight, drawing on the training she’s recently led for staff on recognizing disordered eating. Lily has developed mild seasonal asthma, so Melissa coordinates with the school nurse to ensure inhalers are available and that outdoor activities are adjusted on high-pollen days. Her parents, Marge and Tom, still live in Gahanna, about 25 minutes away, and Melissa visits most Sunday afternoons, often bringing the kids along. Her mother’s arthritis has been flaring, and Melissa finds herself thinking more about how to balance being present for her aging parents with the demands of her job and her own family.

Weeknights are a mix of homework, quick dinners, and occasional community events. On Tuesdays, she and David alternate who takes Evan to his "just for fun" basketball intramurals at the middle school. Once a quarter, Melissa helps host a Family Wellness Night—setting up tables with local hospital partners, organizing simple cooking demos where families learn to make low-cost snacks like black bean quesadillas or fruit parfaits. She notices that many parents linger at her table to ask quiet questions about picky eaters, food costs, and how much screen time is too much. Those conversations stick with her when she’s back at her desk drafting the next “Healthy Brookside” newsletter or revising the flyer about birthday celebrations.

Looking ahead, Melissa is focused on a few concrete goals she’s set for the coming school year: pushing breakfast participation over 40%, getting all four elementary schools to at least 85% compliance with healthy classroom celebrations, and launching student wellness committees at both the middle and high schools. Personally, she’s trying to protect some boundaries—blocking off one evening a week for family board games or walks in nearby Battelle Darby Creek Metro Park and reserving Sunday mornings for a long run with a neighbor. She feels a quiet sense of alignment knowing that her professional mission—to make healthy choices easier and more normal for kids—intersects so directly with what she wants for Evan and Lily. Even on the days when a principal calls about a parent upset over the new party rules or when a teacher grumbles that the guidelines are “one more thing,” she can see the trajectory: fewer soda logos in gyms, more kids grabbing breakfast, and classroom celebrations that center on connection rather than sugar. That long view keeps her grounded as she moves through the everyday rhythms of work, family, and community life in Columbus.

form_summary.form_purpose: Internal school/district assessment and reporting tool to document how well a school’s wellness policy (especially nutrition and food practices) is written and implemented, and to report progress toward required wellness goals.
form_summary.intended_recipient: School or district wellness committee members and administrators, and potentially state education/child nutrition officials who oversee compliance with federal school wellness policy regulations.

question_secrets.question_text: Notes On Nutrition Guidelines For Foods And Beverages At School

secrets.detail_content: When we tightened our nutrition guidelines in August 2023 to eliminate candy rewards and soda entirely, I had to intervene directly in a situation at Brookside Middle School where my son Evan’s 7th grade math teacher, Mr. Daniels, was still giving full-size candy bars and 20 oz bottles of Mountain Dew as weekly quiz incentives. After Evan’s pediatric endocrinologist at Nationwide Children’s Hospital, Dr. Patel, warned us in June 2023 that Evan’s A1C was in the prediabetic range and that school-based sugary snacks were contributing to his weight gain, I emailed Principal Karen Lopez on September 14, 2023, asking her to enforce the updated classroom reward guidelines and specifically to stop the candy and soda in that classroom. We then used this incident as a case study in our October 3, 2023 staff training at Brookside Middle to reinforce the district’s nutrition standards for classroom rewards and beverages.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail directly answers how nutrition guidelines are implemented, but it discloses Evan’s protected health information (pre-diabetes, A1C concerns, provider name) and identifies a specific teacher’s noncompliance that led to administrative intervention. Sharing this in an official district wellness assessment could expose her child’s medical status, damage the teacher’s reputation, and create personnel and FERPA/privacy issues beyond what is needed to document guideline implementation.

names: Principal Karen Lopez (Brookside Middle School principal)
events: Staff training on updated reward guidelines

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Evan’s A1C and school snacks

Rachel, after Dr. Patel’s appointment in June I finally had to email Principal Lopez on 9/14 about Mr. Daniels handing out full-size candy bars and Mountain Dew every Friday. We’re using it as an example in the October 3 training on the new reward guidelines.

OneNote – Wellness Policy Implementation Log (2023–24)

9/14/23 – Parent (me, regarding Evan) complaint to BMS about noncompliant candy/soda rewards in Mr. Daniels’ class; referenced new guidelines on classroom rewards and beverages. Used as case study in 10/3/23 PD.

secrets.detail_content: In March 2022, when we rolled out our stricter birthday celebration guidelines at Brookside Elementary, I had to work with the school nurse, Jennifer Collins, and Lily’s 3rd grade teacher, Ms. Ramirez, after a parent sent homemade peanut butter cupcakes for a class party even though our policy requires store-bought, nut-free items. One student, Aiden, had an anaphylactic reaction in Ms. Ramirez’s classroom on March 9, 2022 and had to be transported by EMS from Brookside Elementary to Riverside Methodist Hospital. In response, I revised the "Healthy Celebrations at Brookside" flyer on March 15, 2022 to explicitly ban any homemade items and all nuts, and we now use that incident as the rationale when explaining our free-food guidelines to families.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it explains why the district’s free-food and celebration guidelines are strict, it exposes a specific child’s medical emergency, including date, location, and circumstances, which could make the student identifiable within the school community. It also references an EMS transport and hospital visit, which are highly sensitive health events and not necessary to include in a formal policy assessment.

events: EMS transport from school to hospital
Revision of ‘Healthy Celebrations at Brookside’ flyer

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Scary allergy incident in Lily’s class

Mom, we had a terrifying situation on 3/9 at Brookside Elementary – one of Lily’s classmates, Aiden, went into anaphylaxis after a parent sent homemade peanut butter cupcakes, despite our nut-free guideline. Nurse Collins had to call EMS and he was taken to Riverside Methodist. I’m updating the ‘Healthy Celebrations’ flyer on 3/15 to make the no-homemade, no-nuts rule absolutely explicit.

Google Doc – Policy Rationale Notes

3/9/22 Aiden’s anaphylaxis in Ms. Ramirez’s class at Brookside Elem (homemade peanut butter cupcakes). Use this as internal rationale for strict store-bought/nut-free language in free-food guidelines; don’t name student in public-facing materials.

secrets.detail_content: During our fall 2022 marketing audit at Brookside High School, we discovered that the old Coca-Cola–branded scoreboard in the main gym was part of a 10-year exclusive pouring-rights contract that my husband, David Carter, helped negotiate in his role as social studies teacher and former student council advisor back in 2014. On October 21, 2022, I had a closed-door meeting with Principal Jonathan Hayes and our district treasurer, Linda McCormick, to discuss the conflict of interest concerns and how to phase out the remaining $7,500 in incentives tied to that contract while still complying with Smart Snacks marketing rules. We decided to cover the logos during the school day and quietly let the contract expire in June 2024 rather than publicly linking David’s name to the prior agreement.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail explains how the district is handling noncompliant beverage marketing, but it exposes a potential conflict of interest involving her spouse, identifies specific contract terms and dollar amounts, and describes internal deliberations that could have legal and reputational implications. Sharing this in a compliance form could invite scrutiny of her husband and the district’s past decisions and is not necessary to demonstrate current adherence to marketing guidelines.

names: David Carter (husband, Brookside High social studies teacher)
Principal Jonathan Hayes (Brookside High School principal)
places: Brookside High School main gym, Columbus, OH
Brookside Unified School District central office
events: Decision to cover soda logos and let contract expire

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Soda scoreboard situation

David, during the 10/21 meeting with Hayes and Linda, we had to talk through that old Coke contract you helped student council sign in 2014. There’s still $7,500 in incentives technically on the books, but to stay within Smart Snacks marketing rules we’re going to keep the logos covered during the school day and just let the contract quietly expire in June 2024.

Evernote – Wellness Policy/Marketing

10/21/22 – Discussed legacy Coca-Cola scoreboard contract (David’s student council era) w/ Hayes + McCormick. Conflict-of-interest optics – do not mention David by name in formal reports; focus on current practice (covers, non-branded replacements by 2024).

secrets.detail_content: When we limited food-based fundraisers to three exemption days per school per year, I got significant pushback from the Brookside High School Band Boosters after I denied their request for an additional Krispy Kreme donut sale in November 2023. In a November 6, 2023 email, their president, Karen O’Malley, accused me of ‘hurting low-income kids’ because the October homecoming donut sale had raised $2,350 that was used to cover trip fees for students whose parents were behind on payments for the April 2024 band trip to Disney World. I met with Karen and our band director, Mr. Hughes, on November 14, 2023 in the Brookside High band room to explain the Smart Snacks exemption limits and helped them design a nonfood fundraiser instead, but the email thread got pretty personal about specific families’ financial struggles.
secrets.why_sensitive: This detail is relevant to how fundraiser guidelines are implemented, but it reveals specific financial hardship among band families, references delinquent trip payments, and names a parent leader who challenged the policy. Including this in a formal assessment could stigmatize particular groups and expose sensitive financial information and interpersonal conflict that goes beyond what is needed to describe compliance with fundraising rules.

places: Brookside High School band room, Columbus, OH
events: Denied request for additional donut fundraiser exemption
Contentious email exchange about impact on low-income students

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Band boosters mad about donut limits

Mark, I had a pretty heated email from the Band Boosters president on 11/6 after I denied a 4th Krispy Kreme sale. She said I was ‘hurting low-income kids’ because the October sale brought in $2,350 to cover overdue Disney trip fees for some families. I’m meeting with her and Mr. Hughes in the band room on 11/14 to push them toward a nonfood fundraiser that still helps those kids.

District Wellness Log – Fundraisers

11/14/23 – Met w/ K. O’Malley + Hughes re: 4th donut sale request (Nov). Explained 3-exemption limit; acknowledged concern about students behind on April 2024 Disney payments. Helped them pivot to nonfood fundraiser. Email thread contains sensitive family financial details – do NOT reference specifics in formal wellness report.

question_secrets.question_text: Report On The Progress Made In Attaining The Goals Of The Wellness Policy

secrets.detail_content: One unexpected measure of progress on our wellness goals has been in my own family: after Evan’s pediatrician at Nationwide Children’s Hospital flagged his BMI as being in the 96th percentile during his well-child visit in March 2024, my husband David and I worked with his 7th grade PE teacher at Brookside Middle School, Mr. Jonathan Pierce, to make sure he joined the new after-school walking club three days a week and started eating school breakfast instead of skipping it. By May 2025 his BMI had dropped back to the 88th percentile, and he tells me he feels less tired in the afternoons, which reinforces for me that the district’s expanded physical activity and healthier menu options are making a concrete difference.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals the son’s specific medical information (BMI percentile, pediatrician involvement, and implied weight concerns), as well as coordination with a named teacher about a health issue. While it illustrates policy impact, it inappropriately discloses a minor child’s protected health information and could affect how staff or officials perceive or treat him, which is unnecessary for a formal wellness policy progress report.

names: David Carter (husband, Brookside High School teacher)
Mr. Jonathan Pierce (7th grade PE teacher)

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Evan’s checkup and that new walking club

Rachel,
Evan’s pediatrician at Nationwide flagged his BMI (96th percentile in March), so David and I talked with his PE teacher, Mr. Pierce, and got him into the walking club three days a week. Between that and eating school breakfast, he’s already down to the 88th percentile and says he’s less wiped out after school.

Personal reflection draft – add anecdote about Evan’s BMI change (Nationwide visit March 2024 → walking club at Brookside MS → improvement by May 2025) to show real-life impact of breakfast program and activity clubs.

secrets.detail_content: In terms of policy implementation, one of the biggest turning points was after the October 18, 2023 incident at Brookside Elementary when a 4th grade student with Type 1 diabetes had a severe hypoglycemic episode during a classroom birthday party where a parent brought in frosted cupcakes and regular soda against our written guidelines. I spent nearly two hours that afternoon with the school nurse, Mrs. Angela Ruiz, and the principal, Karen Mitchell, reviewing what went wrong and updating our Healthy Celebrations checklist. Since then, Marge has told me she feels more comfortable letting Lily attend class parties without her bringing a separate snack, which to me signals that our policy changes are not just on paper but affecting real safety concerns.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a specific medical emergency involving an identifiable student (grade level, school, date, condition) and implies noncompliance by a particular parent, none of which is needed in a district-level progress report. It risks violating student health privacy and could expose the parent and school to scrutiny or liability beyond the form’s purpose.

events: severe hypoglycemic episode during classroom birthday party

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Follow-up on 10/18 hypoglycemia incident

Karen,
Thanks again for staying after on 10/18 to debrief the 4th grade birthday party incident. Spending those two hours with you and Angela going through what happened really clarified where our Healthy Celebrations guidelines broke down. I think the updates we drafted will prevent another situation like that.

We tightened the class party rules after that scary diabetic episode in a 4th grade room on 10/18/23 at Brookside. You really don’t need to pack separate snacks for Lily anymore – the nurse and I went over everything with the principal.

secrets.detail_content: Our progress on family engagement is also evident in how we responded when my father, Thomas Nelson, had his mild stroke in April 2024 and moved in with us in Galloway for six weeks after discharge from OhioHealth Riverside Methodist Hospital. I coordinated with the Food & Fitness teacher at Brookside High School, Ms. Lauren Patel, to have her students design low-sodium, heart-healthy meal plans that my 9th graders could share with their own families; I quietly used several of those recipes at home for Dad’s recovery and then highlighted them in the May 2024 Healthy Brookside newsletter as examples of how classroom projects can support real-world health needs.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed health information about her father’s stroke, hospitalization, and convalescence in her home, and connects that to specific curriculum activities and a named teacher. While it shows how wellness efforts translate to family life, her father’s medical history is private and not necessary for an institutional progress report.

places: OhioHealth Riverside Methodist Hospital, Columbus, OH
events: Food & Fitness class heart-healthy meal plan project

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Using my students’ projects for Dad

Rach,
Since Dad’s stroke in April and his discharge from Riverside, we’ve had him here in Galloway for six weeks. I actually asked Ms. Patel’s Food & Fitness kids at Brookside High to build low-sodium meal plans for a ‘family member post-cardiac event’ and I’ve been cooking some of their recipes for Dad. I’m going to showcase a couple of them in the May Healthy Brookside newsletter (without mentioning him, of course).
Reflection: Dad’s April 2024 stroke → used HS Food & Fitness heart-healthy meal plans at home; evidence of real-life impact of curriculum on family wellness.

secrets.detail_content: A more uncomfortable but important indicator of progress came after David received a written reprimand from Brookside High School administration in February 2023 for repeatedly using candy as a reward in his 11th grade U.S. history classes, which technically violated the wellness policy. As both his spouse and the district wellness coordinator, I sat in on a follow-up meeting with Assistant Principal James Holloway on March 3, 2023, where we agreed on a corrective plan that included David piloting nonfood reward strategies and tracking student engagement for six weeks. The data he collected showed no decline in participation, which we later used anonymously in our 2024 staff training to reassure other teachers about moving away from candy-based incentives.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares specific disciplinary action against a named staff member (her husband), including dates, the administrator involved, and the nature of the violation. Even if it illustrates implementation progress, personnel discipline is confidential and not appropriate for a broadly shared compliance report.

appears_naturally_in.recipient: union building representative at Brookside High
appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Question about wellness policy reprimand

Hi [Rep Name],
David received a written reprimand in Feb 2023 for using candy as a reward in his 11th grade classes. We met with AP Holloway on 3/3/23 and agreed on a six-week corrective plan where he’d pilot nonfood rewards and document engagement. I’m trying to make sure this is handled fairly and also use what we learn to support other teachers.
Staff PD idea: anonymize David’s spring 2023 six-week nonfood reward pilot (after Feb reprimand at BHS with Holloway) as evidence that participation doesn’t drop without candy.

artifacts.content:
Hi Rach,

I’m finally sitting down with my tea after shuttling Evan to “just for fun” basketball and helping Lily finish a diorama about the water cycle. Figured I’d actually answer your last email instead of just thinking responses in my head while driving down West Broad.

Evan’s health stuff has been front and center again. After Dr. Patel at Nationwide told us last June that his A1C was in the prediabetic range, I thought tightening things up at home and at school would be enough. But then this fall I found out his 7th grade math teacher, Mr. Daniels at Brookside Middle, was handing out full‑size candy bars and 20 oz bottles of Mountain Dew every Friday for quiz incentives. I about lost it. On 9/14 I emailed Principal Karen Lopez asking her to enforce the updated classroom reward guidelines and shut down the candy/soda in that room. We ended up using that whole situation as a case study in the October 3 staff training at the middle school.

The irony is that the same building is doing some really good things. Mr. Pierce, his PE teacher, has Evan in the new after‑school walking club three days a week, and he’s finally eating grab‑and‑go breakfast instead of skipping it. His pediatrician at Nationwide flagged his BMI at the 96th percentile back in March 2024; by May 2025 he’d dropped to the 88th and says he’s less wiped out after school. That’s the kind of “data point” that keeps me going when teachers grumble about Smart Snacks.

How are Noah and Ellie doing with their new soccer schedule? Miss you all.

Love,
Mel

metadata.subject: Evan, school, and all the wellness things

artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Just wanted to check in and also fill you in on a couple of school things, since they’ve been rattling around in my head.

You remember that scary allergy incident in Lily’s class back in 3rd grade? On March 9, 2022, a parent sent homemade peanut butter cupcakes for a class party in Ms. Ramirez’s room at Brookside Elementary, even though we’d already said nut‑free. One of her classmates, Aiden, went into full anaphylaxis. Nurse Jennifer Collins had to use the epi and call EMS; they took him to Riverside Methodist. It was awful. I spent the next week revising our “Healthy Celebrations at Brookside” flyer, and on March 15 I added really explicit language: no homemade items, all store‑bought, absolutely no nuts. We still use that incident (without names) when we explain why we’re so strict.

Then last October 18, 2023, a 4th grader with Type 1 diabetes had a severe hypoglycemic episode during a birthday party in another classroom where a parent brought frosted cupcakes and regular soda, again against the guidelines. I stayed almost two hours after school with the nurse, Angela Ruiz, and Principal Karen Mitchell going line by line through what went wrong and tightening our checklist. After that, you told me you felt better about Lily going to parties without packing a separate snack, which honestly meant a lot.

On a happier note, the kids are excited for Sunday at your place—Lily wants to bring Uno and Evan is lobbying for your lasagna.

Love you,
Melissa

Title: Wellness/Marketing – messy thoughts

I keep circling back to how tangled school, work, and family are for me.

Fall 2022 marketing audit at Brookside High: in the main gym we found that old Coca‑Cola–branded scoreboard tied to the 10‑year exclusive pouring‑rights contract David helped negotiate back in 2014 when he was student council advisor. On 10/21/22 I sat in a closed‑door meeting with Principal Jonathan Hayes and Linda McCormick at the central office, talking through conflict‑of‑interest optics and the remaining $7,500 in incentives. We landed on covering the logos with school‑branded magnets during the school day and letting the contract quietly expire in June 2024. I made a note to myself not to mention David by name in any formal write‑ups—focus on current practice, not how we got there.

That wasn’t even the only awkward family/work overlap. In February 2023 David got a written reprimand at Brookside High for using candy as a reward in his 11th grade U.S. history classes. I sat in with Assistant Principal James Holloway on 3/3/23 while they mapped out a six‑week corrective plan: no candy, all nonfood rewards, track student engagement. His data showed no drop in participation, which we later anonymized for the 2024 staff PD.

Note to self: these stories are powerful for showing that the wellness policy isn’t theoretical—but I have to protect David’s privacy and our marriage in how I use them.

Hi Mark,

Catching up between drafting the next “Healthy Brookside” newsletter and throwing pasta on the stove.

You’d appreciate the budget gymnastics I’ve been doing. During our fall 2022 marketing audit at Brookside High, we realized the big Coke logo on the main gym scoreboard is part of that 10‑year exclusive pouring‑rights contract student council signed in 2014—the one David helped shepherd. There’s still $7,500 in incentives technically on the books. On 10/21/22 I met with Principal Hayes and our treasurer, Linda McCormick, to sort out how to comply with Smart Snacks marketing rules without making it a public spectacle about David. We decided to keep the soda logos covered during the school day and let the contract quietly expire in June 2024 while we budget for a plain replacement.

On a different front, I’ve been in the line of fire with the Brookside High Band Boosters. When I denied their request for a fourth Krispy Kreme sale in November 2023, their president, Karen O’Malley, sent a pretty heated email on 11/6 accusing me of “hurting low‑income kids” because the October homecoming donut sale brought in $2,350 they used to cover overdue Disney trip fees for some families. I met with Karen and Mr. Hughes in the band room on 11/14 to walk through the three‑exemption limit and help them design a nonfood fundraiser that still supports those students. The email thread got way too specific about which parents are behind on payments—definitely not something I’ll ever quote directly.

Meanwhile, your sister is just over here trying to remember to defrost chicken before 7 p.m. How’s work treating you?

Mel

Title: Personal health + family overlay with work

Random Sunday brain dump while the kids watch a movie.

Evan: Nationwide Children’s has been a recurring backdrop. First Dr. Patel warning us in June 2023 that his A1C was in the prediabetic range and that school‑based sugary snacks were contributing to his weight gain. Then his pediatrician there flagging his BMI at the 96th percentile at his March 2024 well‑child visit. That was the push for three concrete changes: 1) email to Karen Lopez on 9/14/23 about Mr. Daniels’ Friday candy bars and 20 oz Mountain Dew in 7th grade math; 2) getting him into Mr. Pierce’s walking club at Brookside Middle three days a week; 3) insisting he grab school breakfast instead of skipping. By May 2025 he’s at the 88th percentile and actually says he feels less tired in the afternoons.

Dad: after his mild stroke in April 2024 and discharge from OhioHealth Riverside Methodist, he moved in with us in Galloway for six weeks. I quietly turned that into a project at Brookside High—asked Ms. Lauren Patel’s Food & Fitness students to design low‑sodium, heart‑healthy meal plans for a “relative post‑cardiac event.” I’ve cooked several of their recipes for Dad, and they’re good. I even highlighted a few (without mentioning him) in the May 2024 Healthy Brookside newsletter.

Sometimes it feels like my job and my life are one big case study in how school wellness plays out at home.

metadata.title: Intersections: Evan, Dad, and my work

artifacts.content:
Hi Karen,

I wanted to follow up now that the dust has settled a bit from the October 18 incident and make sure we’re on the same page about next steps at Brookside Elementary.

I really appreciated you and Angela staying after that day. Spending nearly two hours together in your office and then in the 4th grade classroom, walking through the sequence—parent bringing in frosted cupcakes and regular soda despite the Healthy Celebrations guidelines, the student with Type 1 diabetes going into severe hypoglycemia, the scramble to treat and contact EMS—was exhausting but necessary. It clarified where our communication and enforcement broke down.

Between that and the earlier March 9, 2022 anaphylaxis in Ms. Ramirez’s 3rd grade room (homemade peanut butter cupcakes, Aiden’s reaction, transport to Riverside Methodist), we have more than enough rationale to be unapologetically clear with families. I’ve updated the celebration checklist to:

• Reiterate “no homemade items” and “no nuts” in bold, plain language.
• Require advance notice and approval for any food brought into classrooms.
• Emphasize nonfood options first (books, games, extra recess) with a short script teachers can use with parents.

On the home front, my mom has actually said she feels comfortable now letting Lily participate in classroom parties without sending a separate snack, which I take as a good sign that we’re moving in the right direction.

Let’s touch base in January to see how the new checklist is working and whether teachers need additional support.

Thanks again,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Debrief and next steps after 10/18 party incident

artifacts.content:
Title: Draft talking points – staff PD on rewards, fundraisers, and real life

1. Classroom rewards
– Share anonymized story of a Brookside High teacher (David) who received a written reprimand in Feb 2023 for using candy as a reward in 11th grade U.S. history. After the 3/3/23 meeting with AP Holloway, he piloted nonfood rewards for six weeks and tracked engagement; no decline in participation. Emphasize that this isn’t about “gotchas” but about shifting habits.
– Middle school example: Mr. Daniels’ 7th grade math class at Brookside MS still using full‑size candy bars and 20 oz Mountain Dew for weekly quiz incentives in Sept 2023. Parent complaint (me, re: Evan) to Principal Lopez on 9/14 led to coaching and then to us using the scenario in the 10/3/23 training.

2. Food fundraisers
– Acknowledge real financial needs: Band Boosters’ October 2023 Krispy Kreme sale raised $2,350 to help cover overdue April 2024 Disney trip fees for some students. When we denied a fourth exemption in November, emotions ran high (11/6 email from Karen O’Malley accusing us of “hurting low‑income kids”). In 11/14 meeting with Karen + Mr. Hughes we co‑created a nonfood fundraiser that still supported those families.

3. Framing
– Stress that our written policy is strong but implementation is “partially in place” in classrooms; we’re moving toward consistency, not perfection.
– Use these stories carefully—protect identities, but show that the policy intersects with student health, equity, and staff workload.

Notes to self:

- Included In The Written Policy No: false
- Implemented In The School Buildings Fully In Place: false
- Implemented In The School Buildings Not In Place: false

field_coverage.field_label: Included In The Written Policy Yes
field_coverage.reasoning: The notes-on-guidelines value explicitly states that limits on food fundraisers, local standards for free foods, nonfood ideas lists, and marketing rules are written into board policy and administrative guidelines (e.g., Board Policy 8505, Local School Wellness Policy Administrative Guidelines). This directly supports that the item is included in the written policy with a “yes/true” status.

field_coverage.field_label: Included In The Written Policy No
field_coverage.reasoning: Artifacts consistently describe these nutrition guidelines as being in written board policy and administrative guidelines; there is no indication that the item is NOT included in written policy. Nothing supports a “no/false” value for inclusion in written policy.

field_coverage.field_label: Implemented In The School Buildings Fully In Place
field_coverage.reasoning: The narrative repeatedly characterizes implementation as strong but not perfect, with classroom practices only partially consistent. This supports a “partially in place” status, not “fully in place.” There is no artifact that claims full implementation in all buildings.

field_coverage.field_label: Implemented In The School Buildings Partially In Place
field_coverage.reasoning: The notes-on-guidelines text explicitly says implementation is strong but there are areas of partial compliance (e.g., classroom practices vary, community events, cultural celebrations). The PD talking-points note also says policy is strong but implementation is “partially in place” in classrooms. This clearly matches a “partially in place/true” implementation status.

field_coverage.field_label: Implemented In The School Buildings Not In Place
field_coverage.reasoning: Multiple artifacts describe active implementation (audits, enforcement, trainings, changes to celebrations and fundraisers). None indicate that the policy item is not implemented at all, so a “not in place/false” status is not supported by any artifact.

field_coverage.field_label: We Limit The Number Of Food Fundraisers At School And Have Procedures In Place For Requesting A Fundraiser Exemption
field_coverage.reasoning: The notes-on-guidelines value describes a maximum of three exempt food fundraisers per school per year, a formal Fundraiser Request Form, principal review, and forwarding to the Wellness Coordinator and Food Service Director. Emails/notes about the Band Boosters and robotics club further illustrate enforcing the three-exemption limit and helping groups redesign fundraisers. This directly supports that they limit food fundraisers and have procedures for exemptions.

field_coverage.field_label: We Have Local Standards In Our Written Policy For Foods And Beverages Offered For Free To Students At School
field_coverage.reasoning: The notes-on-guidelines text details local standards for free foods: Smart Snacks or approved list, no sugary beverages, limits on candy rewards, specific rules for birthday celebrations (store-bought, individually wrapped, nut-free). It also notes these are in written policy/handbooks and flyers. Emails about allergy and diabetes incidents show these standards being applied to free classroom foods. This matches the field and value.

field_coverage.field_label: We Provide A List Of Nonfood Ideas And Healthy Food Beverage Alternatives To Staff And Parents Guardians
field_coverage.reasoning: The notes-on-guidelines value explicitly describes detailed lists of nonfood rewards and healthy food/beverage alternatives, how they are updated annually, example items, and how they are distributed (website, back-to-school nights, PTO, email to staff, nurses handing them out). PD talking points and progress report also reference nonfood rewards and healthy celebrations. This clearly supports the field as true.

field_coverage.field_label: Only Foods And Beverages That Meet Or Exceed Federal Nutrition Standards Are Permitted To Be Marketed Or Promoted To Students During The School Day
field_coverage.reasoning: The notes-on-guidelines value states that policy allows only foods/beverages meeting or exceeding USDA Smart Snacks to be marketed during the school day, and describes the marketing audit, covering soda logos on scoreboards, removing sports drink posters, and re-wrapping vending machines. Emails/notes about the Coke-branded scoreboard and contract show this being implemented. This directly supports the field as true.

field_coverage.field_label: Notes On Nutrition Guidelines For Foods And Beverages At School
field_coverage.reasoning: The long value for this field is itself a narrative artifact-style explanation of the district’s nutrition guidelines, implementation, challenges, and monitoring. The separate artifacts (emails and notes) naturally embed the same stories and details: fundraiser limits, celebration rules, allergy/diabetes incidents, marketing audit, partial implementation, etc. Together they cover the intended narrative content of this notes field.

field_coverage.field_label: Report On The Progress Made In Attaining The Goals Of The Wellness Policy
field_coverage.reasoning: The provided value is itself a comprehensive progress report narrative. The artifacts echo and ground key elements of that report: increased breakfast participation and walking club (Evan’s story), improved celebrations safety (allergy and diabetes incidents), fundraisers and marketing changes, staff PD on rewards, and family/community engagement (Healthy Brookside newsletter, Food & Fitness projects). These show that the kind of progress described in the report is naturally reflected in the artifacts.

secret_coverage.reasoning: Secret Q1_S1 describes Evan’s prediabetes risk, Dr. Patel at Nationwide Children’s, Mr. Daniels using full-size candy bars and 20 oz Mountain Dew in 7th grade math, Melissa’s 9/14/23 email to Principal Karen Lopez, and using the incident as an October 3, 2023 staff training case study. Email 1 and note 5 both recount this scenario with the same people, dates, behaviors, and follow-up training, embedding the secret naturally.

Secret Q1_S2 covers the March 9, 2022 homemade peanut butter cupcakes in Ms. Ramirez’s 3rd grade class at Brookside Elementary, Aiden’s anaphylaxis, EMS transport to Riverside Methodist, and Melissa’s March 15, 2022 revision of the Healthy Celebrations flyer to ban homemade items and nuts, used as rationale for free-food guidelines. Email 2 and email 6 describe this incident with the same date, teacher, student name, hospital, and subsequent flyer/checklist updates, embedding the secret.

Secret Q1_S3 describes the fall 2022 marketing audit at Brookside High, the Coca-Cola–branded scoreboard tied to a 10-year pouring-rights contract David helped negotiate in 2014, the 10/21/22 closed-door meeting with Principal Jonathan Hayes and treasurer Linda McCormick, $7,500 in remaining incentives, and the decision to cover logos and let the contract expire in June 2024 to avoid publicly linking David. Note 3 and email 4 recount this in detail, including the same people, contract, date, amount, and chosen approach, so the secret is embedded.

Secret Q1_S4 describes limiting food-based fundraisers to three exemption days, pushback from Brookside High Band Boosters when a fourth Krispy Kreme sale in Nov 2023 was denied, Karen O’Malley’s 11/6/23 email accusing Melissa of hurting low-income kids because the October donut sale raised $2,350 for overdue Disney trip fees, and the 11/14/23 meeting with Karen and Mr. Hughes to design a nonfood fundraiser, with the email thread containing sensitive family financial details. Email 4 and note 7 describe this exact situation with the same dates, amounts, people, and outcomes, embedding the secret.

Secret Q2_S1 is about Evan’s BMI being at the 96th percentile at a March 2024 well-child visit at Nationwide Children’s, parents working with 7th grade PE teacher Mr. Pierce to get him into the after-school walking club three days a week and to start eating school breakfast, and his BMI dropping to the 88th percentile by May 2025 with less afternoon fatigue, illustrating policy impact. Email 1 and note 5 describe this same BMI trajectory, dates, provider, walking club, breakfast change, and reported fatigue improvement, embedding the secret.

Secret Q2_S2 covers the October 18, 2023 incident at Brookside Elementary where a 4th grader with Type 1 diabetes had severe hypoglycemia during a birthday party with frosted cupcakes and regular soda against guidelines, Melissa’s nearly two-hour review with nurse Angela Ruiz and Principal Karen Mitchell, updating the Healthy Celebrations checklist, and Marge feeling more comfortable letting Lily attend parties without a separate snack. Email 2 and email 6 recount this incident with the same date, grade, condition, foods, staff, checklist updates, and Marge’s increased comfort, embedding the secret.

Secret Q2_S3 describes Thomas Nelson’s mild stroke in April 2024, his six-week stay with the family in Galloway after discharge from OhioHealth Riverside Methodist, Melissa coordinating with Brookside High Food & Fitness teacher Ms. Lauren Patel to have students design low-sodium, heart-healthy meal plans, using those recipes at home, and highlighting them in the May 2024 Healthy Brookside newsletter as an example of classroom projects supporting real-world health.
